statement: [arXiv:1408.3034 Three-cylinder construction (qualitative existence of a developable Möbius band)] Take three circular cylindrical rods, one of whose diameters is equal to the sum of the diameters of the other two; in the symmetric case considered later these diameters are d, d, and 2d. For suitable dimensions and placement of a rectangular, completely inextensible paper strip, the strip can be threaded between the rods so as to form a Möbius band. The resulting surface consists of three planar sections and three semicylindrical sections. Consequently the constructed Möbius band is developable in the strict sense.
proof_sketch: The elementary local move is that a strip can lie in one plane tangent to a circular cylinder, wrap halfway around the cylinder, and then lie in the other parallel tangent plane; the wrapped piece is a half-cylinder and the remaining pieces are planar, so no stretching is involved. Sadowsky concatenates three such half-cylinder transitions using three rods, with one rod's diameter equal to the sum of the other two. This threading closes the strip with a single half-twist, giving Möbius rather than annular connectivity. Since the surface is assembled only from planar patches and cylindrical patches, each patch has zero Gaussian curvature and is isometric to a planar rectangle; the joins occur along rulings/tangent lines and do not introduce stretching. The later metric formulas specify dimensions in the symmetric d,d,2d case and give the admissible width.

statement: [arXiv:1408.3034 Metric identities for the symmetric d,d,2d construction, equation (1)] In Sadowsky's symmetric construction with three rods of diameters d, d, and 2d, assume that the projections of the midlines of the rectangular planar portions onto the drawing plane lie on the sides of an equilateral triangle PQR of side length L, and that the axes of all three rods are parallel to the drawing plane. Let A,B,C,D,E,F be marked points on the midline, and let A′,B′,C′,D′,E′,F′ be their projections to the drawing plane. Let AB, BC, etc. denote arclengths measured along the midline, and let A′B′, B′C′, etc. denote the corresponding straight projected lengths in the drawing plane. Suppose AF, BC, and DE are the rectilinear planar midline sections, while AB, CD, and EF are helical midline sections on the cylindrical pieces, each helical curve meeting the cylinder generators at an angle of 60°. Then A′B′ = AB cos(60°), AB cos(30°) = πd, hence AB = 2πd/√3 and A′B′ = πd/√3. The two helical sections on the rods of diameter d satisfy EF = CD = πd/√3 and E′F′ = C′D′ = πd/(2√3). The straight planar sections have lengths AF = BC = L − (√3 πd)/2 and DE = L − πd/√3. The total length l of the midline is l = 3L. The marked points can be chosen in the order A-B-C-D-E-F whenever 3√3 πd ≤ 2l.
proof_sketch: For the helical portion on the cylinder of diameter 2d, the strip makes a half-turn, so the transverse/circumferential distance traveled is half the circumference, πd. Since the helix makes angle 60° with the generators, this transverse component is AB sin(60°)=AB cos(30°), giving AB=2πd/√3. Its projected length along the drawing-plane side is the generator-direction component AB cos(60°)=πd/√3. For each cylinder of diameter d, the half-circumference is πd/2; the same 60° helix geometry gives CD=EF=(πd/2)/cos(30°)=πd/√3 and projected lengths C′D′=E′F′=(πd/√3)cos(60°)=πd/(2√3). The projected planar straight pieces are the remainders of the sides of the equilateral triangle after subtracting adjacent projected helical pieces: AF and BC each lose πd/√3+πd/(2√3)=√3πd/2, while DE loses two small-cylinder contributions totaling πd/√3. Summing AB+BC+CD+DE+EF+FA with these formulas cancels all d-terms and gives l=3L. The cyclic ordering requires the residual straight lengths to be nonnegative; the restrictive condition is AF=BC≥0, namely L≥√3πd/2, equivalently 3√3πd≤2l.

statement: [arXiv:1408.3034 Width bound and limiting aspect ratio, equation (2)] In the same symmetric construction, after l and d have been chosen and the six marked points placed as above, mark the generators of the cylindrically twisted parts on the developed strip at angle 60° to the midline. Let 2b be the full width of the rectangular strip. To prevent portions of the band wound around different cylinders from overlapping, allowing at most contact at the boundary, the half-width b is bounded by the shortest straight portions AF=BC. Sadowsky obtains b ≤ (BC/2) tan(60°), equivalently b ≤ l/(2√3) − 3πd/4. Thus the admissible d satisfy d ≤ 2l/(3√3π). Consequently, for every l>0 and every b with 0<b<l/(2√3), one may choose d>0 small enough so that b ≤ l/(2√3) − 3πd/4, and the construction gives a developable Möbius band of midline length l and full width 2b. Equivalently, the construction gives developable Möbius bands with aspect ratios l/(2b)>√3, and these ratios approach √3 as d↓0 and b↑l/(2√3).
proof_sketch: In the flat development of the strip, the ruling/generator lines of adjacent cylindrical pieces enter each straight planar section from opposite ends at angle 60° to the midline. If the half-width is b, moving from the midline to a boundary along such a generator has longitudinal run b/tan(60°). Thus two adjacent cylindrical pieces can coexist without overlap along a straight section of length s provided 2b/tan(60°)≤s, or b≤(s/2)tan(60°). Since AF=BC are shorter than DE, the controlling case is s=BC. Substitute BC=L−√3πd/2 and l=3L: b≤(1/2)(l/3−√3πd/2)√3 = l/(2√3)−3πd/4. Requiring a nonnegative allowable width gives d≤2l/(3√3π), the same condition that keeps A-F and B-C in order. Conversely, if b<l/(2√3), choose any positive d≤(4/(3π))(l/(2√3)−b); then the inequality holds and the Sadowsky construction applies. Taking d arbitrarily small makes the maximal width tend to l/√3, so l/(2b) tends down to √3.

statement: [arXiv:www_ams_org_ae7b650cbc18 Lemma 9.1] Fix a width parameter h>0. For each strip length ℓ>0 there exist a margin δ=δ(ℓ)>0, an angle Θ=Θ(ℓ)>0, and a smooth isotopy ψ: [0,ℓ]×[-h,h]×[0,Θ] → ℝ³ through isometric embeddings with the following properties. Write ψ_t(x,y)=ψ(x,y,t), identify (x,y) with (x,y,0) in ℝ³, and let P_x(a,b,c)=a. (1) ψ_0(x,y)=(x,y,0). (2) ψ_t(x,y)=(x,y,0) whenever x<δ; thus a front margin is held fixed. (3) For x>ℓ−δ, ψ_t(x,y)=T_t(R_t(x,y,0)), where T_t is a translation depending smoothly on t and R_t is rotation about the x-axis through angle t; thus a back margin undergoes a twist about the original lengthwise direction, plus a translation. (4) If δ<x<ℓ−δ and ψ_t(x,y)=(a,b,c), then δ<a<P_x(ψ_t(ℓ−δ,0)); the part between the two margins remains horizontally between them. (5) There is an error function ε(ℓ) with ε(ℓ)→0 as ℓ→0 such that for all (x,y,t), if ψ_t(x,y)=(a,b,c), then (b²+c²)^{1/2}<h+ℓ ε(ℓ). (6) There is a function A(ℓ) with A(ℓ)→0 as ℓ→0 such that Θ(ℓ)=(1+A(ℓ))ℓ/h. In particular, a short length ℓ of strip can be converted, by an embedded isometric motion supported away from a fixed front margin, into approximately ℓ/h radians of twist while staying inside a cylinder of radius h+o(ℓ).
proof_sketch: The basic device is a smooth pseudofold. Choose a smooth nonnegative bump ρ on ℝ with ∫ρ=1, and for each fold angle u let γ_u be a unit-speed planar curve whose curvature is uρ; outside the support of ρ it is straight, with the two straight rays making angle u. Crossing γ_u with a line gives an isometric embedding of a strip which is flat on both sides of a narrow crease-neighborhood and differs there by a rigid rotation. Compositions with Euclidean motions and reparameterizations give pseudofolds along arbitrary straight pseudocrease lines; the support width is the fudge factor. To build the twisting unit, draw three pseudocrease lines l₁,l₂,l₃ in [0,ℓ]×[-h,h], dividing the strip into rigid regions A,C,E,G separated by bend regions B,D,F. For a parameter u∈[0,π/2−small], set the first and third pseudofold angles equal to u. Construct f¹ on the left part by folding along l₁, and g¹ on the right part by folding along l₃. Let X,Y be the endpoints of the middle segment l₂ and compare the image vectors f¹(Y)−f¹(X) and g¹(Y)−g¹(X). Rotate g¹ about the x-axis by the angle that aligns their yz-projections. The geometry of the crease pattern, with a small angle α satisfying 2α<π, ensures the relevant normals are not opposite, so there is a unique smooth middle fold angle u₂(u), measured about the common image of XY, making the normal of the left construction agree with the rotated normal of the right construction. After adding this pseudofold along l₂, translate the right construction to match the left at one point of the overlap E. Agreement at one point, agreement of the tangent along XY, and agreement of the normal force agreement on all of E, so the pieces glue smoothly to an isometric embedding. Finally reparameterize by the accumulated rotation w(u) so that the homotopy parameter t is exactly the twist angle of the back margin. As the fudge factor tends to zero, the construction converges to the elementary sharp three-fold model pictured in the paper; this gives embeddedness for sufficiently small fudge factor, the horizontal containment between the two end margins, the radius estimate h+ℓ o(1), and the asymptotic total twist Θ=(1+o(1))ℓ/h.

statement: [arXiv:www_ams_org_ae7b650cbc18 Section 15, unnumbered Sadowsky-type embedding construction] Let M_l denote the flat width-one Möbius strip M_l=([0,l]×[0,1]) / ((0,y)∼(l,1−y)). If l>√3, then M_l admits a smooth isometric embedding into ℝ³.
proof_sketch: The paper gives an explicit folded-paper model, illustrated in Figure 15.1, based on Sadowsky's construction. The crease pattern fits into a width-one strip with total length arbitrarily close to √3 from above, and when folded sharply along the indicated straight lines it forms a polyhedral Möbius strip: each face is planar and congruent to its preimage in the flat strip, and the two ends match with the required half-twist. For a prescribed l>√3, choose the same pattern with small positive gaps/margins so that all crease neighborhoods and end-matching adjustments fit inside [0,l]×[0,1]. Replace every sharp fold by the smooth pseudofold construction of Section 9: each crease is thickened to a very narrow strip on which the surface bends smoothly and isometrically, while the adjacent flat pieces remain related by the same rigid motion as in the sharp model. The pseudofold supports are chosen disjoint and with arbitrarily small fudge factors. Consequently the resulting smooth developable surface is uniformly as close as desired to the sharp folded model on compact sets away from the creases, and has the same net half-twist at the identified ends. Since the sharp model is embedded with positive clearance between nonadjacent pieces after taking l>√3 and positive gaps, sufficiently small pseudofold neighborhoods preserve embeddedness. The smoothed construction descends through the end identification to a smooth isometric embedding of M_l in ℝ³.

statement: [arXiv:www_ams_org_ae7b650cbc18 Theorem 15.1] There exists a constant ε>0 such that the flat width-one Möbius strip M_l=([0,l]×[0,1]) / ((0,y)∼(l,1−y)) can admit a smooth isometric embedding into ℝ³ only if l>π/2+ε.
proof_sketch: Assume, toward contradiction, that there are isometric embeddings k of M_l with l arbitrarily close to π/2. The analysis of Section 13, originally carried out for cylinder inversions, applies to immersions of M_l near this limiting length. After a rotation, the relevant plane P may be taken to be the xy-plane. For l sufficiently close to π/2, the generating line segments of the developable surface are nearly horizontal, their centers are all nearly at one point, and the direction vector φ(t+1/2) is nearly at angle π/2 from φ(t). For each t∈[0,1/2], let E_t be the generating segment corresponding to t and E_{t+1/2} the one half a period away. Because their projections to the xy-plane are nearly perpendicular line segments through nearly the same center, these projected segments meet in a unique point p_t. Let q_t∈E_t and r_t∈E_{t+1/2} be the two points lying above p_t, and define v(t) to be their vertical separation. Embeddedness gives q_t≠r_t, hence v(t)≠0 for all t. The geometry depends continuously on t, so v(t) is continuous on [0,1/2]; since it never vanishes, v(0) and v(1/2) must have the same sign. But the Möbius identification reverses the two segments when t is advanced by 1/2, so the same comparison at the endpoint gives v(1/2)=−v(0). This contradiction rules out embeddings for all l sufficiently close to π/2, yielding some positive ε.

statement: [arXiv:2308.12641 Lemma 1.3 (T)] For every λ>0, let Mλ = ([0,λ] × [0,1]) / ((0,y) ∼ (λ,1−y)) be the flat Moebius band, and let I:Mλ→R³ be a smooth isometric embedding with image Ω. A bend is a straight line segment B′ contained in Ω which cuts across Ω and has endpoints in ∂Ω; its preimage in Mλ is a pre-bend. A T-pattern on Ω is a pair of bends whose supporting affine lines in R³ are perpendicular and intersect. The T-pattern is embedded if the two bend segments are disjoint. Then every smooth embedded paper Moebius band Ω contains an embedded T-pattern.
proof_sketch: First obtain a continuous foliation β of Ω by bends. On the nonzero mean-curvature locus this is the classical ruling of a developable surface: the kernel of the differential of the Gauss map integrates to straight line segments ending on the boundary; no two such bends meet, else the intersection would have two straight directions and zero mean curvature. Planar components are foliated by interpolating between limiting bends. Each pre-bend meets the centerline of Mλ exactly once: if a pre-bend met it more than once, its length would be at least √(1+λ²)>λ, but its image is a chord between two boundary points, so the two boundary arcs between these endpoints would each have length at least that chord length, forcing 2λ>2λ. Thus the bends are parametrized by the centerline circle S¹. Let Υ be the space of ordered unequal pairs (x0,x1) of bend parameters. Compactify Υ by adding two collision points ∂+ and ∂− according as x1 approaches x0 from ahead or behind in the cyclic order; the result is S², and the swap Σ(x0,x1)=(x1,x0) is conjugate to the antipodal map. For a pair of bends u0,u1, propagate an orientation of u0 continuously along the positive arc from x0 to x1 to obtain an orientation of u1; write the resulting unit directions as →u0,→u1 and midpoints as m0,m1. Define F=(g,h):Υ→R² by g=→u0·→u1 and h=(m0−m1)·(→u0×→u1). This is independent of the initial sign choice because both directions change sign. The Moebius monodromy reverses orientation after one circuit, so F extends continuously to S² with F(∂+)=(1,0), F(∂−)=(−1,0), and satisfies F∘Σ=−F. By Borsuk-Ulam, an odd map S²→R² has a zero; since the poles are nonzero, some unequal pair has F=0. At such a pair, g=0 makes the supporting lines perpendicular. The scalar triple product h=0 says the vector between the midpoints lies in the plane spanned by the two directions, hence the two perpendicular supporting lines are coplanar and therefore intersect. The bends are disjoint because they lie in the same bend foliation. Hence they form an embedded T-pattern.

statement: [arXiv:2308.12641 Lemma 3.1] Let {Lt | t∈[0,1]} be a continuous family of oriented affine lines in R³ such that L1 = L0^opp, meaning that L1 is the same underlying affine line as L0 but with the opposite orientation. Then there exist parameters r,s∈[0,1] such that the underlying affine lines Lr and Ls are perpendicular and intersect.
proof_sketch: Regard the parameter interval with endpoints identified as a circle of underlying unoriented lines with nontrivial orientation monodromy: going once around changes the chosen direction by a sign. Repeat the Borsuk-Ulam construction from Lemma 1.3 for ordered unequal parameter pairs on this circle, compactified to a sphere by the two possible collision limits. For a pair, propagate the line orientation along the positive arc between the parameters; the endpoint condition L1=L0^opp is exactly what makes the swap of the two parameters act antipodally on the propagated orientations. If aj is the propagated unit direction of Lj and mj is any point on Lj, set g=a0·a1 and h=(m0−m1)·(a0×a1). The quantity h is independent of the choices of points mj, since adding multiples of a0 or a1 to m0 or m1 does not change the dot product with a0×a1. The map F=(g,h) is continuous on the compactified sphere, takes the two collision values (1,0) and (−1,0), and is odd under the antipodal/swap involution. Borsuk-Ulam gives a non-collision pair with F=0. Then g=0 says the two directions are perpendicular, and h=0 says the two affine lines are coplanar: m0−m1 has no component normal to the plane spanned by a0,a1. Perpendicular coplanar affine lines intersect. The paper also notes that this condition can be phrased as vanishing of the dual dot product of the corresponding points on the Study sphere of oriented lines.

statement: [arXiv:2308.12641 Lemma 1.4 (G)] Let λ>0 and let I:Mλ→R³ be a smooth isometric embedding of the flat Moebius band Mλ = ([0,λ] × [0,1]) / ((0,y) ∼ (λ,1−y)), with image Ω. Suppose Ω contains an embedded T-pattern, i.e. two disjoint bends B′ and T′ whose supporting affine lines in R³ are perpendicular and intersect. Then the aspect ratio satisfies λ>√3.
proof_sketch: Choose the two bends of the embedded T-pattern and, if necessary, interchange them so that B′ does not contain the intersection point of the two supporting lines. Rotate Ω so T′ lies on the x-axis and B′ lies in an open negative ray of the y-axis. Let T=I⁻¹(T′), B=I⁻¹(B′). Cutting Mλ open along the straight pre-bend T gives a Euclidean trapezoid. In a planar lift, T crosses the width 1 of the strip with signed horizontal displacement t, hence ℓ(T)=ℓ(T′)=√(1+t²). The two boundary arcs of Mλ between the endpoints of T are denoted H and D, with D chosen to contain the farther endpoint u of B from the line of T; the sign of t is chosen so that ℓ(H)+ℓ(D)=2λ and ℓ(D)−2t=ℓ(H). Since H′ is a curve in Ω joining the endpoints of the straight segment T′, ℓ(H)=ℓ(H′)≥ℓ(T′)=√(1+t²). Let u′=I(u). The Euclidean triangle Δ with base T′ and vertex u′ has base √(1+t²). Its height is strictly greater than ℓ(B′): B′ lies in a ray perpendicular to the line of T′, and u′ is the endpoint farther from that line. Also ℓ(B′)=ℓ(B)=√(1+b²)≥1 for some horizontal displacement b of B in the flat strip. The elementary estimate used is: if a triangle has base √(1+t²) and height h≥1, then the sum of its two non-base side lengths is at least √(5+t²), with equality only when h=1 and the triangle is isosceles; this follows by reflecting one base endpoint across the horizontal line through the apex and applying the triangle inequality and Pythagoras. Here the height is actually >1 in the needed sense, so the broken side length ∨ from the endpoints of T′ to u′ is >√(5+t²). Since D′ is a boundary curve joining the endpoints of T′ and passing through u′, ℓ(D)=ℓ(D′)≥ℓ(∨)>√(5+t²). Therefore δ(t):=√(1+t²)+√(5+t²)<ℓ(H)+ℓ(D)=2λ, and using ℓ(H)=ℓ(D)−2t also ν(t):=2√(5+t²)−2t<2ℓ(D)−2t=ℓ(D)+ℓ(H)=2λ. Let t0=1/√3. Then δ(t0)=ν(t0)=2√3, δ is increasing for t>0, and ν is strictly decreasing on all R. If t>t0 then 2λ>δ(t)>2√3; if t<t0 then 2λ>ν(t)>2√3; and if t=t0 the strict inequalities still give 2λ>2√3. Hence λ>√3.

statement: [arXiv:2309.14033 Lemma 2.1] Let λ>0 and let Γ_λ = [0,λ]×[0,1]/((0,y)∼(λ,y)) be the flat cylinder. Let I:Γ_λ→ℝ^3 be an embedded twisted paper cylinder in the paper's sense: I is C∞, injective, and arc-length preserving, and the two boundary components of Ω=I(Γ_λ) form a nonsplit link. A bend is a straight Euclidean line segment contained in Ω with one endpoint on each component of ∂Ω. Then Ω has a continuous partition into bends: Ω is the disjoint union of such straight segments, and these segments vary continuously across the cylinder.
proof_sketch: Let U⊂Ω be the set where the mean curvature is nonzero. Classical developable-surface theory says that a smooth flat surface is ruled on its nonzero-mean-curvature locus; in the present cylinder-with-boundary setting each such ruling segment extends until it hits both boundary components, so U is partitioned into bends (this is Lemma 5.1 in the paper, citing Spivak). The bends in U vary continuously: they are disjoint in the embedded surface, and every bend has intrinsic length at least 1, because its preimage joins the two boundary components of Γ_λ. Thus no limiting degeneration occurs, and the bend partition extends continuously to the closure of U. The complement Ω−U consists of flat open trapezoids. For each such trapezoid, two opposite parallel sides lie in ∂Ω, while the other two sides are limiting bends from the boundary of U. A planar trapezoid with this boundary data has the obvious continuous foliation by straight segments joining the two boundary sides; these segments are bends and match the limiting bends on the other two sides. Filling every flat trapezoid in this way extends the continuous bend partition from U to all of Ω. The appendix also observes that for an immersion, the same argument applied in the domain gives a continuous partition of Γ_λ into prebends.

statement: [arXiv:2309.14033 Theorem 1.1] If I:Γ_λ→ℝ^3 is an embedded twisted paper cylinder of aspect ratio λ, where Γ_λ=[0,λ]×[0,1]/((0,y)∼(λ,y)), I is C∞, injective and arc-length preserving, and the two boundary components of I(Γ_λ) form a nonsplit link, then λ>2.
proof_sketch: The proof first uses Lemma 2.1 to continuously parametrize the bends. A prebend is the preimage in Γ_λ of a bend. Parametrize bends by the point t∈ℝ/λℤ where the prebend meets the midline y=1/2, and let s(t) be the slope of this prebend in the flat cylinder. Then f(t)=s(t)−s(t+λ/2) satisfies f(t+λ/2)=−f(t). By the intermediate value theorem, for some t the prebends at t and t+λ/2 have equal slope. These two parallel prebends cut each boundary component into two arcs of length λ/2; their image bends u and v are called a balanced pair. Move by a rigid motion so that u lies in a plane ℝ^2 and v lies in a plane parallel to it, and orthogonally project to ℝ^2 by Π. Put A=Π(u)=u and B=Π(v). Since bends have length at least 1, the planar segments A and B have lengths at least 1. Let F and G be the two boundary components of Ω. Since F and G are linked, Π(F) and Π(G) must intersect. Hence, after choosing suitable arcs F*⊂F and G*⊂G joining corresponding endpoints of u to endpoints of v, the projected arcs C_1=Π(F*) and C_2=Π(G*) intersect. The key planar estimate is Lemma 2.3: if A,B are planar segments with endpoints A_1,A_2 and B_1,B_2, and C_j joins A_j to B_j for j=1,2 with C_1∩C_2≠∅, then ℓ(C_1)+ℓ(C_2)≥ℓ(A)+ℓ(B)≥2. Indeed, an intersection point x splits the arcs, and the two subarcs from x to A_1,A_2 have total length at least ℓ(A), while the two subarcs from x to B_1,B_2 have total length at least ℓ(B). Since projection does not increase length and the balanced arcs have ℓ(F*)=ℓ(G*)=λ/2, one gets λ=ℓ(F*)+ℓ(G*)≥ℓ(C_1)+ℓ(C_2)≥2. Finally, equality is excluded because the class of achievable aspect ratios is open: from any smooth embedded example one can fatten the cylinder slightly to obtain examples with nearby smaller aspect ratio. Thus an example with λ=2 would imply one with λ<2, contradicting the inequality λ≥2. The paper also gives an alternate convex-hull proof: linkedness implies one boundary component meets the convex hull of the other; a bend from that point gives two points in the hull at distance at least 1; the convex hull has the same diameter as the original loop, so that loop has diameter at least 1 and hence length at least 2.

statement: [arXiv:2309.14033 Theorem 1.3] For every ε>0 there exists an embedded twisted paper cylinder of aspect ratio 2+ε whose two boundary components form a Hopf link. Equivalently, for every ε>0 there is a C∞ injective arc-length preserving map I:Γ_{2+ε}→ℝ^3, with Γ_{2+ε}=[0,2+ε]×[0,1]/((0,y)∼(2+ε,y)), such that the two boundary components of I(Γ_{2+ε}) form a Hopf link.
proof_sketch: Begin with one of the limiting folding patterns for aspect ratio 2: a rectangle/cylinder divided into four right isosceles triangular panels that, after the prescribed up- and down-folds and the end identification, form a fourfold wrapping of a right isosceles triangle, with the boundary components arranged as a Hopf link in the resolved model. Sharp folds are replaced by smooth pseudofolds. A pseudofold is constructed by taking a smooth U-shaped curve U that agrees with two parallel line segments near its ends and crossing it with an interval [a,b]. With U parametrized by arclength, U×[a,b] is an isometrically embedded Euclidean rectangle; geometrically it smoothly makes the tight U-turn that replaces a crease while matching planar strips at both ends. Insert very narrow pseudofolds between the planar triangular pieces according to the folding instructions, keeping the triangular pieces nearly planar and separated according to the desired stacking order. Because the pseudofolds can be made arbitrarily narrow and the construction is an arbitrarily small smoothing of the resolved folded model, the resulting smooth surface is embedded and retains the same over/under topology. Its boundary is initially slightly rough or not quite the boundary of a standard flat cylinder, so one trims off small neighborhoods of the rough edges. This trimming produces a smooth embedded isometric flat cylinder; the added pseudofold widths and trimming change the aspect ratio by an arbitrarily small positive amount. Choosing these small parameters appropriately, or equivalently making the construction first with aspect ratio arbitrarily close to 2 and then adjusting by a tiny flat strip, gives the prescribed aspect ratio 2+ε. The boundary link type is unchanged under these small isotopies and trims, so the two boundary components form a Hopf link.

statement: [arXiv:2412.00572 Lemma 3.3 and §3.1 (h(t), d(t) optimality inequality)] Let M_λ be the flat Möbius band obtained from a 1×λ rectangle by identifying the two length-1 sides with opposite orientations, and let I:M_λ→Ω⊂R^3 be a smooth embedded paper Möbius band, i.e. a smooth isometric embedding. A bend is a straight line segment in Ω whose endpoints, and only endpoints, lie in ∂Ω. By Lemma 3.2, Ω has a T-pattern: two disjoint bends B′ and T′ lying in perpendicular intersecting lines. After a rigid motion, assume T′ lies in the X-axis and B′ lies in the negative Y-axis. Let T=I^{-1}(T′), cut M_λ open along T, and normalize the resulting symmetric trapezoid so that the two boundary lines are horizontal; let t be the signed horizontal displacement of the segment T, so that ℓ(T)=sqrt(1+t^2). Then the metric inequality λ > max(h(t),d(t)) holds, where h(t)=sqrt(1+t^2)+t and d(t)=sqrt(5+t^2)-t. Consequently every smooth embedded paper Möbius band has λ>sqrt(3). Moreover, if 0<ε<1/4 and λ<sqrt(3)+ε, then, with t_0=1/sqrt(3), one has |t−t_0|<4ε/3; in particular t∈(0,1).
proof_sketch: Lemma 3.2 supplies the T-pattern: the bend foliation has space of leaves homeomorphic to S^1; the space of ordered unequal bend-pairs has a 2-point compactification homeomorphic to S^2; dot-product and cross-product data give two odd continuous functions on S^2 whose common zero, by Borsuk-Ulam, gives perpendicular intersecting supporting lines. For the inequality, normalize the T-pattern and cut M_λ along T. In the cut trapezoid let H=H_1∪H_2 and D=D_1∪D_2 be the two relevant boundary chains. Flat geometry gives λ=ℓ(H)+t=ℓ(D)−t. Since I is an isometry, ℓ(H)=ℓ(H′), and H′ is a boundary curve joining the endpoints of the chord T′; because T′ is a bend with only endpoints on ∂Ω, ℓ(H′)>ℓ(T′)=ℓ(T)=sqrt(1+t^2). Thus λ>sqrt(1+t^2)+t=h(t). For D, the perpendicular bend B′ gives a triangle in space with base ℓ(T′)=sqrt(1+t^2) and height greater than ℓ(B), while ℓ(B)≥1 in the unit-width flat band. If ∨ is the union of the two non-horizontal sides of this triangle, the elementary minimum for a broken path over height at least 1 gives ℓ(∨)>2 sqrt(1+(ℓ(T′)/2)^2)=sqrt(5+t^2). Since ℓ(D)=ℓ(D′)≥ℓ(∨), λ>sqrt(5+t^2)−t=d(t). Now h is strictly increasing and d is strictly decreasing, and h(1/sqrt(3))=d(1/sqrt(3))=sqrt(3); hence for any t, either h(t) or d(t) is at least sqrt(3), proving λ>sqrt(3). For the quantitative part, h and d are convex, h′(t_0)=3/2 and d′(t_0)=−3/4. If t≥t_0, convexity gives h(t)≥sqrt(3)+(3/2)(t−t_0), so λ<sqrt(3)+ε forces t−t_0<2ε/3. If t≤t_0, convexity gives d(t)≥sqrt(3)+(3/4)(t_0−t), so t_0−t<4ε/3. Combining yields |t−t_0|<4ε/3. Since ε<1/4 and 1/3<t_0<2/3, this implies t∈(0,1).

statement: [arXiv:2412.00572 Lemma 2.3] Assume 0<ε<1/4. Let ▽ be a Euclidean triangle with a horizontal base, and call its non-base vertex the bottom vertex. Let ▽* be the isosceles triangle with the same base and the same height as ▽, obtained from ▽ by moving the bottom vertex horizontally; let d(▽,▽*) be the horizontal distance moved. Let ∨ be the union of the two non-horizontal sides of ▽, and let ∨* be the union of the two non-horizontal sides of ▽*. Suppose ℓ(∨*)<3 and the slopes of the two sides of ∨* have absolute value greater than 1. If d(▽,▽*)≥sqrt(13ε/2), then ℓ(∨)>ℓ(∨*)+2ε.
proof_sketch: Let p_1,p_2 be the base vertices of ▽, q its bottom vertex, and q* the bottom vertex of ▽*. Reflect p_2 across the horizontal line through q and q*, obtaining r_2. By symmetry of ▽*, ℓ(∨*)=ℓ(p_1r_2), while ℓ(∨)=ℓ(p_1q)+ℓ(qr_2). The horizontal displacement of q from the line p_1r_2 is at least sqrt(13ε/2). Since the line p_1r_2 has slope of absolute value at least 1, the perpendicular distance from q to that line is at least ρ=sqrt(13ε/4). Now use the sliding trick: slide q parallel to the line p_1r_2 to a point q′ for which ℓ(p_1q′)+ℓ(q′r_2) is minimized. The minimizer makes triangle p_1q′r_2 isosceles, so if L=(1/2)ℓ(∨*)<3/2, each of the two equal sides has length at least sqrt(L^2+ρ^2)=sqrt(L^2+(13/4)ε). Lemma 2.1 says that, because L<3/2 and ε<1/4, sqrt(L^2+(13/4)ε)>L+ε. Therefore ℓ(∨)=ℓ(p_1q)+ℓ(qr_2)≥ℓ(p_1q′)+ℓ(q′r_2)>2L+2ε=ℓ(∨*)+2ε.

statement: [arXiv:2412.00572 Lemma 2.5] Assume 0<ε<1/4. Let S be a line segment with ℓ(S)<3, and let I:S→R^3 be a unit-speed parametrized curve. Let I*:S→R^3 be the affine map agreeing with I at the two endpoints of S. Write ℓ(I(S)) for the arclength of I and ℓ(I*(S)) for the length of the straight chord image of I*. If sup_{p∈S} ||I(p)−I*(p)|| ≥ 3sqrt(ε), then ℓ(I(S)) > ℓ(I*(S)) + ε.
proof_sketch: Parametrize S by arclength and consider the graphs Γ={(x,I(x))} and Γ*={(x,I*(x))} in R×R^3. If s=ℓ(I*(S))/ℓ(I(S))≤1, then Γ has speed sqrt(2), while Γ* has speed sqrt(1+s^2). Lemma 2.4 gives two key estimates: ℓ(Γ*)≤ℓ(Γ)<3sqrt(2), and ℓ(Γ)−ℓ(Γ*)≤ℓ(I(S))−ℓ(I*(S)). The sup-norm hypothesis gives a point q∈Γ whose vertical R^3-distance from the point of Γ* with the same S-coordinate is at least 3sqrt(ε). Since Γ* has slope at most 1 in R×R^3, the actual Euclidean distance from q to Γ* is at least sqrt(9ε/2). Let L=(1/2)ℓ(Γ*)<3/sqrt(2). Among all broken paths from the endpoints of Γ* through a point at this distance from the line Γ*, the shortest occurs after sliding the point parallel to Γ* until the two subsegments are equal; hence ℓ(Γ)−ℓ(Γ*)≥2sqrt(L^2+(9/2)ε)−2L. Lemma 2.2 says that for L<3/sqrt(2), sqrt(L^2+(9/2)ε)>L+ε/2. Thus ℓ(Γ)−ℓ(Γ*)>ε. Combining with the graph comparison estimate yields ℓ(I(S))−ℓ(I*(S))≥ℓ(Γ)−ℓ(Γ*)>ε.
